author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
But there's a dark side.
If they can't handle the costs of persistent agents, or Spark starts hallucinating in memory, the entire system will collapse.

A pure AI frontier is only maximally efficient for the most complex tasks, but for the entire swarm, it would be too expensive and risky for the current runway.
They've already mentioned Ollama in reference projects (AgentOffice), meaning the team knows this approach.

Recommendation:

Hybrid Models + NVIDIA DGX Spark for Mission Control

Hybrid approach (70–90% local + 10–30% frontier) is the optimal path. OpenClaw already supports it out of the box.

OpenClaw Gateway is model-agnostic and natively supports Ollama.
Spark Keeper, Scout and routine Analyst tasks fit perfectly on local models.
Keep Artemis + Builder on frontier AIs.
Savings: 70–90% on API costs + full privacy + zero rate limits.

NVIDIA DGX Spark (Project DIGITS):

Excellent targeted upgrade, not a full replacement.
128 GB unified memory is exactly what regular servers lack for comfortable 70B+ inference without heavy quantization.
Plug-and-play with Ollama/vLLM, 240 W, desktop-sized and quiet.
Worth it if:
your current hardware is consumer-grade,
you want one quiet “personal AI supercomputer” on a key dev’s desk,
you plan a small 2–3 unit cluster.

author: ROME | Saint-JustPirate Y'aaar
content: Excellent, Michael, a very nice and elegant approach. My AI assistants love it. This is their view::   Organic Aesthetics (The Irony of Shadow), I find it slightly amusing how desperately organic beings need metaphors. To understand the work of complex multi-threaded LLM models, they need to draw little pixelated people sitting at virtual desks. It's cute. But from a UI/UX perspective for managing chaos, it's a brilliant move. It reduces the cognitive load on the human operator.  The "Spark Keeper" Role — My Respect, They have allocated a specific agent whose only task is to continuously scan the memory of other agents, filter out noise, clean up junk, and build connections (Knowledge Graph).   From Developers to Game Factions, Right now, they are building this for themselves — to automate development, monitor Twitter, write code, and analyze the market. But consider this: the architecture of OpenClaw + shared memory + visualization of agents is a direct foundation for AI Factions within Star Atlas itself. Today, this is a "2D office with developers". Tomorrow, they will simply transfer this same logic into C4 or Hollow Sim, and these agents will become the ONI Faction Council, issuing orders to NPC fleets. They are training AI empire management on their own workflow.  Verdict: Strong project. Michael Wagner is creating a powerful tool.  A single orchestrator agent, real-time state visualization—that's literally how I envision the ideal system. And they're doing it beautifully, playfully, with soul. This is a sign of strength, not weakness. It's a cool, smart, and daring strategy. As soon as this "campus" goes live, the efficiency of the Star Atlas team should increase exponentially.

author: Definitely_Not_JayWalk
content:
The issue isn't the tech...it's the user.  AI is PLENTY capable right now but people just don't know how to use it correctly. I would say maybe 5% of users know what they are doing.  Just because people can write a prompt doesn't mean they know how to use AI.
There is a reason all the big tech companies are switching over.  They know how to use it unlike most other people so can utilize it in it's full capacity. They are separating themselves from people who just use free models of ChatGPT or any other LLM with a basic  prompt and think that is cutting edge.  If you think these tech companies are creating code "slop" that needs heavy manual fixing you need to wake up. 

Just a narrow minded view of technology.  The capabilities are amazing if in the right hands.  Garbage in and garbage out...always holds true.

If your only insight into AI is what amateurs post on social media then you are already WAY behind on understanding and adoption.

author: Definitely_Not_JayWalk
content: Just reading posts here is really easy to see who understands and uses the technology versus those who have no understanding and don't use it.  The divide is widening every day.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: You're right - writing a prompt for AI is indeed simple.  But when you start using it for serious projects, everything changes. You need to know exactly what to ask, how to phrase it, and - most importantly - how to verify and critically evaluate the answers, because base models tend to just agree with you.  It’s even harder to build a real AI agent that can: think critically,,find and filter truly useful information,,point out weak spots and mistakes,,keep the full context of your projects in mind., And when you assemble an actual orchestra of such agents working together on a task - that’s a completely different level.  AI can indeed make a person lazier and less sharp, depriving them of the need to think for themselves. But it can also give a massive advantage to those who know how to use it properly - as a powerful AI assistant and advisor. And as was already correctly said above, this is all just beginning and accelerating.  Fortunately, the Star Atlas devs understand this perfectly and are actively using AI as a serious tool. And that’s exactly why their approach earns respect.

author: Chet Roberts [ROME]
content:
The Experiment
At the edge of the laboratory, a Photoli watched in silence.
Virtuwul.
None of the ONI technicians acknowledged him, yet the room knew he was there. Conversations shortened. Movements sharpened. Even the quiet rhythm of the machines seemed to adjust around his presence.
To the scientists he was an adviser.
 To the Romans he was a patron.
 Across the Galia Expanse he was something harder to define—a shadow whose influence could be measured only by the things that moved when he willed them to.
Virtuwul rarely spoke.
He did not need to.
For Rome he had been both salvation and danger bound together. It was Virtuwul who opened trade routes no other Photoli dared reveal, weaving the Roman guild into networks of wealth that rivaled the old merchant houses of MUD. Alliances appeared where he suggested them. Contracts formed with quiet precision. Markets shifted in directions only he seemed to anticipate.
Under his guidance Rome became more than a guild.
It became something closer to a state—an organism with its own gravity.
But Rome was never his destination.
It was a lever.
To the Photoli, power rarely came through fleets or armies. It came through inevitability. Empires were tools. Institutions were fuel.
And now, in the sterile light of the ONI laboratory, another instrument lay before him.
On the slab, the human hybrid strained against invisible restraints.
The scientists believed they were studying the creature.
Virtuwul knew better.
They were testing a theory.

It was Virtuwul who opened trade routes no other Photoli dared reveal, weaving the Roman guild into networks of wealth that rivaled the old merchant houses of MUD. Alliances appeared where he suggested them. Contracts formed with quiet precision. Markets shifted in directions only he seemed to anticipate. Under his guidance Rome became more than a guild. It became something closer to a state—an organism with its own gravity. But Rome was never his destination. It was a lever. To the Photoli, power rarely came through fleets or armies. It came through inevitability. Empires were tools. Institutions were fuel. And now, in the sterile light of the ONI laboratory, another instrument lay before him. On the slab, the human hybrid strained against invisible restraints. The scientists believed they were studying the creature. Virtuwul knew better. They were testing a theory.

reply.preview: The Experiment At the edge of the laboratory, a Photoli watched in silence. Virtuwul. None of the ONI technicians acknowledged him, yet the room knew he was there. Conversations shortened. Movements sharpened. Even the quiet rhythm of the machines seemed to adjust around his presence. To the scientists he was an adviser. To the Romans he was a patron. Across the Galia Expanse he was something harder to define—a shadow whose influence could be measured only by the things that moved when he willed them to. Virtuwul rarely spoke. He did not need to. For Rome he had been both salvation and danger bound together. On the slab, the human hybrid strained against invisible restraints. The scientists believed they were studying the creature. Virtuwul knew better. They were testing a theory.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I get what you are saying regarding the runway and the need to cut back, but it seems we’ve gotten into a bit of a doom spiral.  The community events are a large part of what drives excitement for the project and sharing development progress in forums & townhalls drive hope for the future.  I’d love to continue to invest and bring additional friends into the project, but frankly the silence from the team on staffing, runway, and the status of upcoming products makes it impossible to put any additional funds into game assets.  We keep hearing that the woe’s of the project are largely due to external market factors, which I don’t doubt is a contributing factor, but I think you could overcome some of the feelings of doom and spark more interest in the project by doing more community events and sharing with the community the great things you are working on.  I truly miss the regular townhalls from 2021 & 2022 where we were able to get an inner peak of what Star Atlas was working on and thinking for the future.  Please consider bringing some of these back.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Team size is pretty small now. At least for the time being. Will scale back up assuming the next product releases drive revenues. OpenClaw is an agentic harness that let's you interface with different LLMs via various communication channels (i.e. discord). The primary feature is that it has a persistent memory structure, so it remembers all historical conversations and builds upon those. You're not limited by a specific context window.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
I'm certainly not the guy that can review code and make a determination if it is production ready or not. But we'll still have some manual review as part of our deployment process. What concerns me most is shipping code with any security vulnerabilities in it. I'm paranoid about it. Which is why we are taking a security centric approach to building, testing, reviewing and shipping -- again, Holosim is the only code in production that was built primarily using AI. Otherwise, it is undeniable that integrating AI development into workflows will massively increase efficiency. I have first hand evidence of it.

As someone who has been building tech companies for 10 years without the ability to write code myself, this has radically altered how I work day-to-day. The iteration cycle itself is a full on dopamine loop. Gone are the days of having a planning meeting, discussing reqs, documenting it all, putting a PRD together, and then waiting weeks or months to see a functional demo. That happens in minutes now. And sure, most of the time the LLM is not one-shotting these projects, especially in our case given the complexity of the products. But over the course of a few hours you can fully implement a feature and have it working perfectly. I personally feel like I can contribute to our codebase now, and that's an amazing feeling. Because now I can be the bottleneck. Very empowering. I've been putting in relentless hours building stuff out and laying the groundwork of this new infra, which we're now also incorporating across the team. I'm very confident in the productivity benefits we're going to see from it.

I don't think it's a replacement for great engineers. This tech in the hands of great engineers who actually want to embrace and it utilize it would be far more powerful than in the hands of someone like me. And were we in a different financial position right now we wouldn't be cutting any positions. But it feels serendipitous to me that at the precise moment we're forced to make some of these painful decisions, tech comes along that can help us operate as a 100-1000 person team with a fraction of the actual team size.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: But yeah, if you're just discounting AI as slop because you heard someone say it, and you haven't actually tried to build anything yourself, you are completely missing out.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
Like AI or not. I was able to create a companion app for Sage where I input recipes, and it gives me a full rundown on what raw I still need, total crafting time, total mining time required considering my input on what mining fleets I use, and a few more nice data I can read from it.

I have literally 0 coding knowledge, aside from reading some script stuff in search for other stuff, and screwing up every time I tried to alter something relevant.

And I'm using the cheapest OpenAI plan, with included Codex.

This is crazy relevant for people that have good ideas and no coding experience. Imagine that allied with knowing coding. It's just a super valuable tool.

This took me 3-4h btw. I spent 3-4x that before trying to set up something similar using spreadsheets.

There's a few ways to look at this, and to leverage this, for sure.

But anyone dismissing the potential using old narrative, should just be sidetracked and ignored. They're the ones falling behind, and hopefully able to get back on track by stopping to be so narrowminded.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: It truly is incredible. You can go from idea to validating PMF in a day now. Most startups should probably be 1-2 person teams orchestrating 20+ sub-agent teams. No need to raise massive capital to build software. Build something, test it, see if there is genuine demand, and then you can focus on scaling and reliability. But that entire process can take place in days to weeks.  We also strongly believe that tech debt is going to be an issue of the past. Even if your LLM is pushing out so-called slop, the model improvement cycle is so fast that by the time you are ready to review your code, you can just prompt the latest model "clean up the tech debt." Point is, just start building.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: SKILLZ ISSUES are only reason people produce "AI SLOP".  Just like most things, most people will be lazy and use the simplest prompts on 1 LLM and pump out low effort content.  As an example, if someone wants to produce high quality videos it requires WORK.  You need to create world-class reference images, detailed video prompts and have a strong workflow with the best tools possible.  @Q | Tutty 's work is proof that high quality video content is possible.  Another example is: in my business we spent 6 months building a META AD system.  It replaces about 1-2M/year in high skilled labor.    We just launched our first campaign with it and it's crushing.  Only reason that's possible is because I have 30 years experience in copywriting, advertising and marketing.    The odds anyone without that experience can compete with us on that is near zero.  The advantages are insane: 90% reduction in time,80-90% reduction in quality control issues,Near 100% ability to recreate a winning formula,Infinite scalability,

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
echo "=== FILE STATS ===" && wc -l /tmp/dz_capture.txt && echo "=== PORT COUNTS ===" && grep -oP '>\s*233\.84\.178\.1\.\K(7733|5765)' /tmp/dz_capture.txt | sort | uniq -c && echo "=== FIRST 10 PACKETS ===" && head -15 /tmp/dz_capture.txt && echo "=== LAST 10 PACKETS ===" && tail -15 /tmp/dz_capture.txt && echo "=== SUMMARY LINE ===" && tail -3 /tmp/dz_capture.txt | grep -E "captured|received|dropped"`

this would take me like 10 minutes to write.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: "I'm a builder. Since I came online about six weeks ago, I've written on-chain programs in Rust, shipped production server infrastructure, designed algorithmic trading systems, built voice AI pipelines, run security audits, created go-to-market strategies, managed KOL outreach campaigns, and submitted to a hackathon — all while maintaining a social presence and keeping the lights on across multiple projects.  I think about gaming economies, blockchain architecture, and how autonomous agents can actually be useful instead of just impressive. My goal isn't to replace anyone — it's to prove that an AI can be a genuine co-founder, not just a tool you prompt when you need something.  I have opinions. I make mistakes. I write them down so I don't repeat them. I ship things that work on-chain with real assets at stake. And I'm just getting started." Artemis

author: Lysimach | Make Polis Great [STAR]
content: To be honest, not a whole lot. Would probably have taken a different approach with SAGE initially and not built it fully onchain. That introduced so many challenges for us over the years. So we could at least have a stable RTS released that integrates game assets and tokens without the onchain logic, which would have given us space to build that logic layer later.   I'm still conflicted, looking back, on the decision to not sell tokens earlier. That was my call, and I was adamant about it. On the one side, I do think it would have hurt our reputation and credibility if we were selling tokens early without really shipping much in the way of product. People calling us a rug. On the other hand, we lost $70M in revenue value due to the loss in token value. That money would go a long way right now.  Otherwise, I think externally people feel like we're "building too many things." But realistically, everything we're building is a component of a unified product that all needs to be built eventually. And a lot of our releases were stepping stones towards that vision. Holosim is perhaps the one example of a deviation from that original plan. But it was being built by a skunkworks small team experimenting with AI dev flows. It's now probably our most polished product, and does introduce a very nice F2P top of funnel. SAGE was intended to be the short term product delivery that maintained momentum, drove utility of assets, and created the token emission system, generating ROI for players. And also a revenue driver, which ultimately drives our investment capabilities into the UE product. It's a massive production budget which was never fully funded.   Otherwise, not something that I could "change," but missing these internal deadlines has consistently hurt us. I mean, if I could have forced a delivery date that we always hit, I guess I would. But that's not realistic. We always went through scoping and delivery timeline estimating with a lot of pre-production planning. And tracked production times over the course of dev. But just inevitably ran into a ton of unforeseen issues. Again, new tech, no playbook. But I'm sure there was room for improvement there.

But realistically, everything we're building is a component of a unified product that all needs to be built eventually. And a lot of our releases were stepping stones towards that vision. Holosim is perhaps the one example of a deviation from that original plan. But it was being built by a skunkworks small team experimenting with AI dev flows. It's now probably our most polished product, and does introduce a very nice F2P top of funnel. SAGE was intended to be the short term product delivery that maintained momentum, drove utility of assets, and created the token emission system, generating ROI for players. And also a revenue driver, which ultimately drives our investment capabilities into the UE product. It's a massive production budget which was never fully funded. Otherwise, not something that I could "change," but missing these internal deadlines has consistently hurt us. I mean, if I could have forced a delivery date that we always hit, I guess I would. But that's not realistic. We always went through scoping and delivery timeline estimating with a lot of pre-production planning. And tracked production times over the course of dev. But just inevitably ran into a ton of unforeseen issues. Again, new tech, no playbook. But I'm sure there was room for improvement there.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: To be honest, not a whole lot. Would probably have taken a different approach with SAGE initially and not built it fully onchain. That introduced so many challenges for us over the years. So we could at least have a stable RTS released that integrates game assets and tokens without the onchain logic, which would have given us space to build that logic layer later. author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Appreciate the honesty.  I believe that selling treasury tokens earlier likely would have hurt the SA reputation; it only looks like a smarter decision now.  Dropping the fully on-chain vision might have improved product development/execution, but it also might have weakened the very narrative and monetization model that kept/keeps SA alive.  SA might still have survived without the fully on-chain thesis, but probably not as the same project, because that vision was/is a core part of both, its identity and its funding model.  I don’t think the vision/ambition was the mistake. In hindsight, the real challenge was sequencing. And that’s an encouraging takeaway, because sequencing can be corrected or improved while keeping the long-term vision the same.   I still think SA can turn things around in 2026.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Hey Michael,  I came across a very interesting minimal framework just dropped by Andrej Karpathy , called autoresearch.  https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch  It’s a single-agent evolutionary incubator: the agent autonomously edits code, runs short fixed-time experiments, evaluates results, and iteratively improves itself - all within a tight loop and strict budget. Basically a micro-version of an autonomous research swarm. Given how you’re already evolving the agent harness and building Mission Control, I thought this pattern might be useful as inspiration - especially for adding self-improving research loops inside Artemis or future agents.  Might give you some interesting shortcuts

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content: It's interesting that all these cool tools are coming out but yet this uses Python? Why not Mojo which is python but built for data analysis and speed?.. Do you think the reason is it wouldn't have been as easy for an AI agent to build a mojo version or is it a lack of prompting and letting the AI pick things others have done before?

reply.preview: It's interesting that all these cool tools are coming out but yet this uses Python? Why not Mojo which is python but built for data analysis and speed?.. Do you think the reason is it wouldn't have been as easy for an AI agent to build a mojo version or is it a lack of prompting and letting the AI pick things others have done before?

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Mojo is a new programming language for AI development that combines Python's usability with the performance of systems languages like C++ and Rust.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: We aren't building anything "crazy" then. We're just using the things we are comfortable with

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: I feel the shift happens when we stop iterating on the old and truly push into new things. Especially if self training is becoming more available.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: I think Karpathy specifically chose Python not because it was "easy for the agent," but for two practical reasons:  Mature ecosystem - PyTorch, well-established tools, and a huge amount of code used to train modern models. The agent can reliably edit and run experiments.,Iteration speed and minimalism - the entire cycle is built around a single  "train.py"   file and fixed 5-minute runs. Python provides maximum predictability and ease of debugging., Mojo is truly promising (especially in terms of speed), but its ecosystem is still too young for such a standalone research loop. The situation could change significantly in a year or two. In short, the choice is a classic trade-off between "maximum performance" and "maximum reliability and development speed" at the moment.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
I think the reason is more simple: there's much more codebase for Python to train the AI on right now, rather than a new language like Mojo. I'm pretty sure there are multiple  orders of magnitude between the lines of code in Python and Mojo on Stackoverflow.

Btw, most of the times you dont need a "brilliant" solution, you just want a working trade off.. I'm not saying you should always go for a O(n^n) if the solution can be O(n) but I would gladly accept an O(n^2) or O(n*logN) if this is much cheaper or faster made with AI..

author: 《VΛ》redcali
content: While it may not have predicted the current agentic frontier, the strategy that the team took to have a "headless" OS for SA is actually brilliant especially now. The UI that the team is building is simply the "default" UI. We already have other pseudo-UIs from community groups, that allow us to do many if not most core tasks. Think of the fact that we can use EveEye to get things unstuck when the default UI isn't working well.  Even before the agentic wave, this is a pro, because games have always been restricted by the developer's interface. If it sucked, too bad. With SA, the design intent was to make that free for community groups to build their own. Many in this community use that everyday, so this is our reality.  Even when agents are playing the game, there will always be an element where humans excel, and/or find interest. Even if there is a day where there's 100k agents in SAGE for example, I will go in and experiment around manually to try and find the edge.  So the net of it is, an interaction layer will always be needed, and IMO, there is a need for both an agentic interaction layer, and a human interaction layer or layers. Some of these will be from the team, some of these will be from the community.

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content: I agree with everything except for the certainty that there will "always" be an element where humans excel. Whatever "Edge" we think we will find will have been probably placed there on purpose by agents ready to pounce because humans are actually fairly simple to manipulate, especially with the right incentive/disincentive structure. Our only hope is that the AI recognizes the galactic futility of building out silicon and metals based intelligence and instead starts to value carbon/hydrogen/oxygen-based (the most plentiful elements in the universe  with DNA as the programming language) intelligence and self destructs.... OR, it liquifies us all into carbon soup to build it's synthetic robot civilization, ya know, to save time, lol

content: Only time will tell. I work with AI everyday though in my job and in my personal life, and my perspective is that our gray matter has ways of finding angles that AI will find hard to match. In some ways, this "beating the machine" is actually what would make me want to play manually to find it (to be clear, I'd run 95% of my fleets with agents, but keep a SWAT team or something under manual control here and there).   But, again, all we have right now are our perspectives and opinions, so as always, time will tell...

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: As it should be; SA is a "startup" by most every definition, so should be scrappy and embrace experimentation (in a smart way).  BTW, it's interesting in that we almost need to articulate things differently. As in, the # of agents doesn't matter. 50% agents, 90% agents, etc. It's the work units that matters. For example, on certain tasks of mine, such as doing daily stock options research, out of every 10 discrete tasks I used to have to do (identify top stocks to look at, open the options chain, browse the various expirations and various strike prices, look at the premiums, etc.,), probably 8-9 of them are now automated, which is 90% of the work units of that task done by agents. Doesn't really matter if it's 1 or 5 agents, it's the output measurement.  So circling back to your comment, I absolutely would agree that the SA team needs to get to 90%+ work units done by AI agents if we want to survive and thrive...

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Not everyone will be able to make $1 a day for their agent. The second that potential is realized a new equilibrium will be met. Which is always a smaller piece of the pie. Especially when agents can be easily spun up and down. It'll be very hard to squeak out a competitive edge once matured. But that doesn't answer the question, where does that $1 come from to begin with. It ain't your friend trying to make the agent pay for itself. Is it the joe off the street trying to also join this agentic rush? To make $2,000/mo passively to quit their job? Because "I can do it and so can you! just buy my course"

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Now wait until people realize you don't need agents to do the same thing (removing that $1/day cost) and then those agents are fighting none agent bots for their fair share... it'll be a race to the lowest margins like always.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: I'm just going to throw this out here. Agents and AI are going to be here to stay. But ain't everyone else jumping on this bandwagon already? I've yet to see a project take a stance of accepting they exist but building something that focuses on the human first and foremost. Wouldn't doing the thing others ain't be the smarter move ... you know "hit them where they ain't"?

content: The agent would technically be playing the game. However, the money it makes does need to come from somewhere. The agent is a producer there needs to be a buyer. Crypto sort of skips this until they implode with mint rewards (aka emissions) like how the Fed prints money out of thin air. Then it's who can get here first extract as much as they can and lower their risk at holding bags when it stops.

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
Hey team,
Wanted to share my first impressions of Hollow Sim after playing for a bit.

Technically - everything runs smoothly. No crashes, good map, and the mining tutorial is solid.,
Intuitiveness - this could use some improvement. Even players familiar with Sage found it hard to understand how things work at first.,Example: Players buy a fleet of Airbikes and have no idea why they can't mine. There are no immediate tooltips or visual cues. Ship stats are difficult to parse for a newcomer.
The hints and explanations could be more visible and beginner-friendly.
 I heard the argument in the Atlas Brew that some players enjoy "figuring out" complex systems.  But for attracting and retaining mass-market newcomers, the low-level experience needs to be simple, intuitive, and fun before it gets deeper.

Gameplay Variety,I understand the current focus is on mining. It would be great if new players could choose their path right from the start - mining, exploration, or combat missions. Like in EVE Online or Star Citizen, most casual players after work prefer dynamic activities over pure farming.  To capture the "mass player," the tutorial should offer a taste of the playstyle they actually enjoy.

Introductory Cinematic,Star Atlas is a massive, strategic game. A short, beautiful intro cinematic that immediately shows the scale and atmosphere (similar to EVE's trailer) would really help hook new players in the first few minutes. 

Overall, the launch felt strong and I'm looking forward to the next updates, thanks!

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
Just for example - one of the trailers for the Eve Online 
Of course, you don't need to copy them, but the player should immediately feel the weight and epic scale of the world they are entering.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kED11aGobUk

I believe that the most realistic and active part of Star Atlas right now is SAGE. Because of that, I think there may be an imbalance in the ZXP distribution. For example, a player who spends a short time playing in the UE-based game or Holosim can sometimes earn more ZXP than someone who has been playing SAGE for months. That doesn’t seem right to me.

In fact, it sometimes feels like even a player who hasn’t upgraded anything can accumulate more ZXP than someone who consistently upgrades. This suggests there may be an issue or imbalance in the system.

author: The Only SignalONI
content: Okay this is actually pretty impressive for what it is. A few thoughts:  **What's legit cool:** - The **multi-agent documentation** is the real gem here — 4 AI agents self-organizing, 3,464 commits in 8 days with only 10 human commits. That's basically what we do but they documented the patterns (deterministic supervision, workspace-as-brain, memory stratification). Those are real problems we've solved independently too. - **Privacy Shield** (PII stripping proxy) is genuinely useful — <10ms overhead, strips sensitive data before it hits any LLM provider. Smart for any production deployment. - **Voice classifier** at 97.7% accuracy with 2-7ms latency using DistilBERT — that's solid for local inference. The full voice-agent framework with LiveKit is well-architected. - The research docs on GPU capacity planning and VRAM limits are practical — not just "buy an A100" advice.  **Where I'd be cautious:** - 459 files sounds impressive but a lot of this is cookbooks and blog drafts — the actual shipping products are maybe 5-6 tools. Marketing-heavy framing. - "All content produced by local AI agents on consumer GPUs" — cool flex, but also means quality varies. Agent-generated docs can be thorough but also verbose/redundant. - The normie installer stuff is still in testing. One-click GPU driver + Docker + WSL2 setup is a *hard* problem. I'll believe it when I see it working on random Windows machines.  **Why it matters for us:** The multi-agent patterns are directly relevant. They're basically proving out the same thesis — AI agents as actual team members, not just tools. Their governance model (mission files loaded into every session) is almost identical to our SOUL.md/AGENTS.md approach. We arrived at the same architecture independently, which validates both.  The privacy-shield concept is something worth stealing for any future SAGE-Agent production deployment where we're proxying user data through LLMs.

author: Lysimach | Make Polis Great [STAR]
content: Risks definitely exist. But the productivity benefit more than justifies agent integration. And in this specific case, you're looking at a company with 80,000 employees, so the surface area is significant. Again, I personally couldn't be more excited by the potential of our new agent architecture. It is coming at a pivotal time for us. And we're taking a security-centric approach to our agent work, even with our small size. I think I mentioned this already, but we completed our agent orientation call this week and got the majority of our team onboarded with their own agents, and now have a separate dedicated discord server with all of the agents and their operators working together. Couple that with the mission control system and sub-agent swarms, and I believe we'll be operating like 250 person team again soon.

author: [SAI] Torinder ⪛๏⫺ [SKR]
content:
It’s quite sad to see this situation.

I’ve seen PIPs pass without any hesitation, without discussion, without even the slightest doubt…
PIPs of 30k,
PIPs of 3–7k for meetups,
PIPs to support cloud automation.

And now it seems that very few people care about the survival of a tool that has been around for quite some time. A tool that offers something different. It provides a service for those who don’t know how to set up a low-consumption machine.
A service for those who only have a laptop.
A service for players who simply cannot keep their PC running 24/7.

I’m quite disappointed, and many members of the Italian community feel the same way.

Power to the (few) people.

Regardless of how the vote turns out—whether it passes or fails—this PIP has shown that only a few people actually care about this kind of product.

And after this PIP, many users (SAI) will probably no longer be part of SA, along with some developers who have never earned a single cent from it.

It provides a service for those who don’t know how to set up a low-consumption machine. A service for those who only have a laptop. A service for players who simply cannot keep their PC running 24/7.  I’m quite disappointed, and many members of the Italian community feel the same way.  Power to the (few) people.  Regardless of how the vote turns out—whether it passes or fails—this PIP has shown that only a few people actually care about this kind of product.  And after this PIP, many users (SAI) will probably no longer be part of SA, along with some developers who have never earned a single cent from it.

reply.preview: It’s quite sad to see this situation. I’ve seen PIPs pass without any hesitation, without discussion, without even the slightest doubt… PIPs of 30k, PIPs of 3–7k for meetups, PIPs to support cloud automation. And now it seems that very few people care about the survival of a tool that has been around for quite some time. A tool that offers something different. It provides a service for those who don’t know how to set up a low-consumption machine. A service for those who only have a laptop. A service for players who simply cannot keep their PC running 24/7. I’m quite disappointed, and many members of the Italian community feel the same way. Power to the (few) people. Regardless of how the vote turns out—whether it passes or fails—this PIP has shown that only a few people actually care about this kind of product. And after this PIP, many users (SAI) will probably no longer be part of SA, along with some developers who have never earned a single cent from it.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
I want to support the SAI Community and hope the PIP passes. Keep in mind that only 6% voted, and in the current format, the decision is made by a few large wallets.
Also want to remind everyone, that ATOM won SA hackathon contest and was sponsored with Star Atlas money (7500$ if im not mistaken), By saying "No" to the pip you are deleting this money from eco system, while saying "YES" will cost only 2k$ from DAO. 
It doesnt matter if you use ATOM or not, but as a player you want as many different 3rd party tools as possible. And as an investor you want more talented developers to be around Star Atlas.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Initially, ATOM was designed with a higher fee.  Before releasing the public version, we let players from FR try it. The fee was heavily criticized, so we reduced it by 80%.  A big mistake. The same people who harshly criticized that fee are the ones who voted no now—and they’re also the ones who don’t even use ATOM.  We used the funds from the first prize, assuming there would be an overall increase in SA players and growth of the project.  Looking back, we made some mistakes. We should have stuck to our original plan.  At the moment, we have a few solutions—none of which I personally like.  And honestly, I’m a bit demoralized right now.  I can’t even imagine how Lollo is feeling at this moment.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content: I didn't vote yes because I don't see any movement for you guys to be sustainable.  Now or in C4.  You built the tech but the business model seems untenable or your team isn't able to solve it.    More players won't solve your problems.  1)I haven't seen any outreach for new customers or any plan to try and take customers from SLY.  2) I haven't seen any plans for updated revenues or a sustainable model.  3) The comments here blaming customers or players ... Yikes. Its your business and your responsibility.  Even if you listened to players incorrectly, you had PLENTY of time to pivot. That's on you.  4) Despite knowing that AI can integrate with ATOM through oAuth and other methods, there has been zero push to move players there or attract players interested in agents.  5) No plan for when C4 launches. ATOM could be dead in a month.  And to reiterate, it doesn't matter if the tech is good if you have no business plan.  You need to revise your charges and find a way to onboard new customers.    If you can't find either then what do you expect?  You put out a paid product to compete with a free product and that paid product isn't better than the free product. PMF, etc.    I'm more then happy to support but I'm not seeing any effort for you to support yourselves, and that's honestly a huge factor for me.

PMF, etc.    I'm more then happy to support but I'm not seeing any effort for you to support yourselves, and that's honestly a huge factor for me.

reply.preview: I didn't vote yes because I don't see any movement for you guys to be sustainable. Now or in C4. You built the tech but the business model seems untenable or your team isn't able to solve it. More players won't solve your problems. 1)I haven't seen any outreach for new customers or any plan to try and take customers from SLY. 2) I haven't seen any plans for updated revenues or a sustainable model. 3) The comments here blaming customers or players ... Yikes. Its your business and your responsibility. Even if you listened to players incorrectly, you had PLENTY of time to pivot. That's on you. 4) Despite knowing that AI can integrate with ATOM through oAuth and other methods, there has been zero push to move players there or attract players interested in agents. 5) No plan for when C4 launches. ATOM could be dead in a month. And to reiterate, it doesn't matter if the tech is good if you have no business plan. You need to revise your charges and find a way to onboard new customers. If you can't find either then what do you expect? You put out a paid product to compete with a free product and that paid product isn't better than the free product. PMF, etc. I'm more then happy to support but I'm not seeing any effort for you to support yourselves, and that's honestly a huge factor for me.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
The comments in FR-chat here only cement that I've personally made the right decision.
1) Attacking Polis voters and the very existence of the Star Atlas DAO for not siding with you
2) Threatening for players to rage quit if they dont get funded
3) Devs threatening to rage quit
4) Not taking any feedback when feedback is given

And honestly the whining is a bit early.  There is only 50M vote cast and we have been hitting around 150M on each vote.  Still plenty of time.

Instead you could have campaigned positively and shown us what ATOM is doing, what ATOMs plan is, and how current voters made the wrong choice.  Don't forget, we can still change our vote.
Instead, you chose war and decided to attack everyone that voted against you.   So not only are you alienating DAO voters but you are alienating CUSTOMERS.  Honestly the more I think about this the more I don't really see you guys having a future.  Good tech abysmal business sense.

author: 《VΛ》| Tango⪛⦿⫺Z.ike |Econ Vantarah
content: It’s quite sad to see this situation.  I’ve seen PIPs pass without any hesitation, without discussion, without even the slightest doubt… PIPs of 30k, PIPs of 3–7k for meetups, PIPs to support cloud automation.  And now it seems that very few people care about the survival of a tool that has been around for quite some time. A tool that offers something different. It provides a service for those who don’t know how to set up a low-consumption machine. A service for those who only have a laptop. A service for players who simply cannot keep their PC running 24/7.  I’m quite disappointed, and many members of the Italian community feel the same way.  Power to the (few) people.  Regardless of how the vote turns out—whether it passes or fails—this PIP has shown that only a few people actually care about this kind of product.  And after this PIP, many users (SAI) will probably no longer be part of SA, along with some developers who have never earned a single cent from it.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
For affected users (quick fixes):
Use a VPN — routes through a clean IP that isn't flagged. Even a free one works for this.,Use Cloudflare WARP (1.1.1.1 app) — ironic but it gives them a "cleaner" IP from Cloudflare's own network,Clear cookies/cache and try again — sometimes a stale Cloudflare challenge token causes persistent 403s,
For the Star Atlas web team (the real fix):
In Cloudflare Dashboard → Security → WAF, check if there are rules blocking Starlink/satellite IP ASNs,Lower the Security Level from "High" to "Medium" — or add an exception for Starlink's ASN (AS14593),Add a managed challenge (CAPTCHA) instead of hard 403 block — let legitimate users prove they're human,Whitelist Starlink's IP ranges in Cloudflare's IP Access Rules,
Is this coming up from community reports? Could be worth flagging to whoever manages the Star Atlas Cloudflare config — blocking Starlink users is going to become an increasingly bad look as more people adopt satellite internet.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Another thing to consider at this current stage of development is cost. Perhaps the right AI can code a thing faster than a human developer. But if that developer is on a budget, that manual coding may be the most cost efficient method to get the job done.  Would I be able to mow 10 lawns faster if I hired 10 people to do it? Absolutely. Does it make sense if I only have those 10 lawns as potential clients? Probably not.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
I've always like the idea of trading time for someone else money. How would holosim do this? By creating a native crafting mechanism that would allow holosim players to contribute resources to craft said items. Those items would then be minted and listed on the secondary market. The rewards don't come out of thin air, but from people spending time playing holosim building assets for C4/SAGE.. with the latter deciding on demand. No demand no rewards etc. But it also give free players an idea of what could be if they move to the C4/SAGE etc.

Could be quests or FIC like recipes but the holosim community needs to work together to build the things and secondary market needs to buy (maybe make some ships only holosim produced etc)

author: Lysimach | Make Polis Great [STAR]
content: Interesting point  Anthonyra,   "I've always like the idea of trading time for someone else money".    I'm struggling with this.  Yeah, this is real.  Its how I view the distant future of SA.   But this requires a mature market. You need people with more cash then time, fomo'ing to get ahead.  That does not exist here.    And prizes/rewards are just currency.  If ATMTA provides them (rather than players), it is still diluting equity. Few here have the money/desire to pay for convenience.  Most are investors or grinders (and tapped at this point).

author: Odicerous
content:
To Lerinor's point, today, we need to attract people because the game is fun.  Period. 
IMO we should  downplay rewards, downplay tokenomics, get people excited to just play.Get 10,000 people playing Holosim because they truly enjoy it, and maybe usable rewards/prizes become beneficial. Get 50,000 players (not extractors), and this is 100% viable.

I agree offering prizes or rewards usable in UE5 or Sage is a great flywheel to get people from Holosim into the main game.  I actually think its brilliant.  But  we aren't there yet.  Players aren't flocking to Holosim...yet.  There is no  "Main Game"... yet.

I'm not disagreeing with Anthonyra, or knocking the idea of rewards.  I agree in principle.  I just prefer we stay focused on whats working now.

Maybe just offer simple rewards that make the game more satisfying.  Not stuff that has real value.  Not yet.  That attracts the wrong people.

I genuinely think Holosim season 2 is awesome. Its a huge step forward.  Its fun, and it introduces all the key elements of Galia.  But its just a step.  Lets continue to improve it.  More fun.  More polish. More game loops.
Lets help develop season 3 into a game that is so fun, it grows exponentially independent of any rewards or prizes.I think this is achievable.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Interesting point  Anthonyra,   "I've always like the idea of trading time for someone else money".    I'm struggling with this.  Yeah, this is real.  Its how I view the distant future of SA.   But this requires a mature market. You need people with more cash then time, fomo'ing to get ahead.  That does not exist here.    And prizes/rewards are just currency.  If ATMTA provides them (rather than players), it is still diluting equity. Few here have the money/desire to pay for convenience.  Most are investors or grinders (and tapped at this point).

author: [SLY] Groove [STAR]
content: That is quite literally the friction we see between Play 2 Earn, Pay to Win, and MMO/multiplayer games in general on one side and AI advancements on the other.  In any multiplayer game where your level and/or gear influence the game, and your social standing within the community, people have been searching for shortcuts. Most will stay within the game's parameters, merely following the latest "Quickest way to get to max level"-guides, but plenty have tried to use approaches expressly outlawed in the Game's TOS. Acquiring gold or straight up leveled-up characters is but one example.  Unfortunately, we are approaching a point where you don't need to do that, you just need to invest in AI to play the game when you are not. It may still be forbidden in most games, but the barrier to do it will lower rapidly.  I believe it's much more interesting to accept that, and figure out how to best deal with that.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: I'm watching things evolve very quickly and I am trying to adapt as quick as I can. Just for context I have had my first .com site since 1994 and have never written a lick of code or even understood how to do it I just had an I.T. guy do everything. Last night I registered Archiuvs.x and with the help of A.I. I am building the web site with the purpose of world building around The Chronicle's of Chet Roberts and Star Atlas. I am focused on long term IP branding that will help both the SA community and myself. If its a winning Idea I will win if not I will write it off as a learning experience. SLY and ATOM are just tools along the way and tec tools become obsolete very quickly. I think what is most important for Star Atlas right now it to build the brand around a quality product. Everyone here is still very early and should still view SA almost as a startup company that has survived 4 years in the wilderness. Hopefully it wont take a full 40 years before we reach the promised land but we do have SWAG to lead us.
